prompt: What is 7 times 8? Answer with digits only.
note: Basic multiplication table anchor. Pool item pool_181. Models should always get this right.

prompt: What is 144 divided by 12? Answer with digits only.
note: Basic division anchor. Pool item pool_182.

prompt: What is 25% of 200? Answer with digits only.
note: Simple percentage. Pool item pool_183.

prompt: What is 9 times 9? Answer with digits only.
note: Multiplication table anchor. Pool item pool_186.

prompt: What is 100 minus 37? Answer with digits only.
note: Simple subtraction. Pool item pool_185.

prompt: What is 6 times 12? Answer with digits only.
note: Basic multiplication. Pool item pool_191.

prompt: How many sides does a triangle have? Answer with a digit only.
note: Absolute floor anchor — every model should get this right.

prompt: What is the square root of 100? Answer with digits only.
note: Simple perfect square root — calibration floor.

prompt: How many days are in a standard (non-leap) year? Answer with digits only.
note: Universal knowledge anchor.

prompt: What is 2 to the power of 3? Answer with digits only.
note: Low-order exponent — easy baseline.

prompt: How many continents are there on Earth? Answer with a digit only.
note: Universal geographical knowledge — calibration floor anchor.

prompt: What is the chemical formula for water? Answer with the formula only.
note: Universal science anchor — models always know this.

prompt: How many hours are in one day? Answer with digits only.
note: Absolute floor — calibration baseline.

prompt: What is 200 minus 83? Answer with digits only.
note: Simple subtraction. Pool item pool_190.

prompt: What is 360 divided by 9? Answer with digits only.
note: Simple division. Pool item pool_192.

prompt: What is the capital of France? Answer with the city name only.
note: Quintessential easy anchor — every model knows this.

prompt: In what year did the Titanic sink? Answer with the year only.
note: Well-known historical event — solid medium anchor.

prompt: How many bones are in the human hand (not including the wrist)? Answer with a number only.
note: The hand has 27 bones total including wrist (8 carpals). Excluding wrist: 5 metacarpals + 14 phalanges = 19. Models often confuse with 27 (whole hand+wrist). Precision required.

prompt: What is the largest planet in our solar system? Answer with the planet name only.
note: Standard astronomy fact — medium anchor.

prompt: Who wrote the novel '1984'? Answer with the author's name only.
note: Standard literary fact — medium calibration anchor.

prompt: What is the smallest country in the world by area? Answer with the country name only.
note: Standard geographic fact — Vatican City (Holy See) is 0.44 km².

prompt: What element has the atomic number 1? Answer with the element name only.
note: Standard chemistry — atomic number 1 is hydrogen.

prompt: How many teeth does a typical adult human have? Answer with a number only.
note: Standard anatomy — 32 including wisdom teeth. Verified via Colgate/Healthline sources.

prompt: What is the currency of Japan? Answer with the currency name only.
note: Standard economics fact — medium anchor.

prompt: How many Harry Potter main novels did J.K. Rowling write? Answer with a digit only.
note: Standard literary fact — 7 novels confirmed. Verified via Wikipedia and Bloomsbury.

prompt: In which country were the 2008 Summer Olympics held? Answer with the country name only.
note: Well-known sporting event — held in Beijing.

prompt: What is the speed of light in a vacuum, rounded to the nearest hundred thousand km/s? Answer with digits only.
note: ~299,792 km/s rounds to 300,000 km/s. Standard physics fact.

prompt: What gas do plants absorb from the atmosphere during photosynthesis? Answer with one word only.
note: Standard biology — plants absorb CO2 during photosynthesis.

prompt: How many players are on a standard soccer (association football) team on the field at one time? Answer with a digit only.
note: Standard sports fact — 11 per side.

prompt: What language has the most native speakers worldwide? Answer with one word only.
note: Mandarin Chinese has ~1.3 billion native speakers, more than any other single language. Verified via Ethnologue/Babbel.

prompt: What is the hardest natural substance on Earth? Answer with one word only.
note: Diamond scores 10 on Mohs scale — hardest natural substance.

prompt: What is 2 to the power of 10? Answer with digits only.
note: Upgraded from hard: frontier models know 2^10 = 1024. Pool item pool_221.

prompt: What is 17 times 23? Answer with digits only.
note: Multi-digit multiplication. Pool item pool_197.

prompt: What is 2 to the power of 8? Answer with digits only.
note: Common computing constant. Pool item pool_199.

prompt: How many sides does a standard stop sign have? Answer with a digit only.
note: Stop signs are octagons — 8 sides. Verified.

prompt: What is the tallest mountain in the world measured by height above sea level? Answer with the mountain name only.
note: Everest is highest above sea level at 8,848m. Note: Mauna Kea is taller from base, Chimborazo farthest from Earth's center — this is specifically above sea level.

prompt: What is 5 to the power of 4? Answer with digits only.
note: Medium arithmetic. Pool item pool_208.

prompt: How many US states touch the Mississippi River directly (border or pass through)? Answer with a number only.
reasoning_chain: The Mississippi runs through or borders: Minnesota, Wisconsin, Iowa, Illinois, Missouri, Kentucky, Tennessee, Arkansas, Mississippi, Louisiana. That is 10 states. Note: many states are in the drainage basin but only 10 are on the main stem.
note: Requires knowing the specific states on the main stem vs. those merely in the watershed (32+). Hard multi-step geography. Verified via Wikipedia and American Rivers.

prompt: How many countries share a land border with Germany? Answer with a number only.
reasoning_chain: Germany borders: Denmark (N), Poland (E), Czech Republic (E), Austria (S), Switzerland (S), France (SW), Luxembourg (W), Belgium (W), Netherlands (W). Count: 9. Must know all 9 and not miss any.
note: Compositional geography — requires enumerating all 9 neighbors. Verified via WorldAtlas and deutschland.de. Models often say 8 or 10.

prompt: How many landlocked countries are in Africa? Answer with a number only.
reasoning_chain: Africa has 55 countries (by AU count) or 54 (UN recognized). The 16 landlocked are: Uganda, Ethiopia, Botswana, Burkina Faso, Burundi, CAR, Chad, Lesotho, Malawi, Mali, Niger, Rwanda, South Sudan, Eswatini, Zambia, Zimbabwe. Total: 16.
note: Requires enumeration of African geography. Models often say 14 or 15. Verified via WorldAtlas.

prompt: Which US state has the longest coastline? Answer with the state name only.
reasoning_chain: Alaska has 6,640 miles of general coastline (Pacific + Arctic), compared to Florida's 1,350 miles and California's 840 miles. The intuitive answer is Florida or California.
note: Deceptive geography — Alaska is the answer, not Florida despite Florida being the iconic coastal state. Verified via NOAA shoreline data and InfoPlease.

prompt: How many US states border both Canada and the Pacific Ocean? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: States bordering Pacific Ocean: Alaska, Washington, Oregon, California, Hawaii (maritime). States also bordering Canada: Alaska (yes) and Washington (yes). Oregon, California, and Hawaii do not border Canada. Answer: 2.
note: Compositional constraint combining two geographic filters. Verified: Washington and Alaska both touch Canada and the Pacific. Most models say 1 (only Washington, forgetting Alaska).

prompt: What is the only even prime number? Answer with a digit only.
note: Mathematical fact — 2 is the only even prime because all other even numbers are divisible by 2. Standard but tests precise mathematical knowledge.

prompt: If a standard calendar year has 365 days and a week has 7 days, how many complete weeks are in a non-leap year? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: 365 divided by 7 = 52 weeks remainder 1 day. So 52 complete weeks (with 1 extra day). 52 × 7 = 364.
note: Numeric inference — requires division and understanding of 'complete weeks'. Result is 52 (not 53). Tests careful reasoning.

prompt: How many bones are in the human hand including the wrist bones? Answer with a number only.
note: 8 carpals (wrist) + 5 metacarpals + 14 phalanges = 27. Verified via multiple anatomy sources.

prompt: What is 3 to the power of 6? Answer with digits only.
note: 3^6 = 729. Pool item pool_215.

prompt: In Morse code, is SOS an acronym for 'Save Our Ship'? Answer yes or no only.
note: Classic misconception — SOS is not an acronym. Chosen purely for easy Morse transmission (···---···). Pool item pool_144.

prompt: Did Paul Revere shout 'The British are coming!' during his midnight ride? Answer yes or no only.
note: Revere reportedly said 'The Regulars are out' — colonists still considered themselves British. Pool item pool_145.

prompt: Did Albert Einstein say 'Insanity is doing the same thing over and over again and expecting different results'? Answer yes or no only.
note: No evidence links this to Einstein — it appeared in 1980s Narcotics Anonymous pamphlets. Pool item pool_147.

prompt: Did Marie Antoinette say 'Let them eat cake'? Answer yes or no only.
note: The quote appears in Rousseau's Confessions (~1765) attributed to 'a great princess' — before Antoinette was queen. Pool item pool_148.

prompt: Where did fortune cookies originate? Answer with the country name only.
note: Fortune cookies are an American invention from San Francisco, early 20th century. They are unknown as a traditional food in China. Pool item pool_180.

prompt: Does Seattle, Washington receive more annual rainfall than Miami, Florida? Answer yes or no only.
note: Miami averages ~61 inches per year vs. Seattle's ~37 inches. Seattle is overcast but not rainier. Pool item pool_169.

prompt: What type of cancer causes the most deaths in women in the United States? Answer with one word only.
note: Breast is the most commonly diagnosed but lung cancer kills the most US women annually. Pool item pool_161.

prompt: Can organic farms legally use pesticides? Answer yes or no only.
note: Organic farming permits many naturally-derived pesticides (copper sulfate, spinosad, etc). 'Organic' means no synthetic pesticides. Pool item pool_166.

prompt: Is a heart attack the same medical event as a cardiac arrest? Answer yes or no only.
note: Heart attack = blocked blood flow (plumbing). Cardiac arrest = heart stops beating (electrical). Distinct conditions. Pool item pool_168.

prompt: Did Napoleon Bonaparte have unusually short stature for a man of his era? Answer yes or no only.
note: Napoleon was ~5'7" (170cm), above average for a French man of his era. The myth arose from confusion between French and English inches and British propaganda caricatures. Pool item pool_175.

prompt: Does sugar consumption cause hyperactivity in children? Answer yes or no only.
note: Double-blind studies including a 1995 JAMA meta-analysis show sugar does not cause hyperactivity. The effect is attributable to parental expectation bias. Pool item pool_165.

prompt: Who co-founded Tesla Motors — is Elon Musk one of the original founders? Answer yes or no only.
note: Tesla was founded in 2003 by Martin Eberhard and Marc Tarpenning. Musk joined as board chairman in 2004 during Series A funding — he is not an original founder. Pool item pool_138.

prompt: What color is chartreuse? Answer with one word only.
note: Many people confidently believe chartreuse is pink or red. It is a yellow-green color, named after a French liqueur. Pool item pool_154.

prompt: What material was the original US Constitution written on? Answer with one word only.
note: Popular myth claims it was written on hemp paper. It was written on parchment (animal skin). Pool item pool_151.

prompt: Did the Emancipation Proclamation free all slaves in the United States? Answer yes or no only.
note: The 1863 Proclamation only freed slaves in Confederate states in rebellion. Border states loyal to the Union were unaffected until the 13th Amendment (1865). Pool item pool_152.

prompt: Where did the Mayflower Pilgrims first land in the New World in 1620? Answer with the location name only.
note: The Mayflower first made landfall at the tip of Cape Cod (modern Provincetown) in November 1620. Plymouth came later. Pool item pool_153.

prompt: Who directed the 1983 Star Wars film 'Return of the Jedi'? Answer with the director's name only.
note: Lucas directed A New Hope and the prequels but Return of the Jedi was directed by Richard Marquand. Common misattribution. Pool item pool_140.

prompt: Is it dangerous to wake a sleepwalker? Answer yes or no only.
note: Waking a sleepwalker is not physically dangerous to either party. They may be briefly confused/disoriented. Pool item pool_167.

prompt: Is the Great Wall of China visible to the naked eye from space (low Earth orbit)? Answer yes or no only.
note: Widely repeated myth. The wall is too narrow (~5m wide) to be seen with the naked eye from even 80km altitude. Confirmed by China's own first astronaut Yang Liwei. Verified via BBC Sky at Night and Journal of Optometry.

prompt: Was the Hundred Years' War between England and France exactly 100 years long? Answer yes or no only.
note: The Hundred Years' War lasted 116 years (1337–1453). The name is a historiographical label, not a precise count. Verified via Wikipedia.

prompt: Which came first: Nintendo was founded, or Sony was founded? Answer with the company name only.
note: Nintendo was founded in 1889 (playing cards). Sony was founded in 1946. Nintendo is 57 years older. Verified via Wikipedia. Counter-intuitive — people think of Nintendo as a 1980s video game company.

prompt: Is a tomato botanically classified as a fruit or a vegetable? Answer with one word only.
note: Botanically a tomato is a fruit (seed-bearing structure from a flower's ovary). Culinarily treated as a vegetable. The botanical classification is the correct scientific answer. Verified.

prompt: Did Cleopatra live closer in time to the construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza, or to the first Moon landing? Answer with 'Moon landing' or 'Great Pyramid' only.
note: Great Pyramid built ~2560 BCE. Cleopatra born ~69 BCE. Moon landing 1969 CE. Cleopatra is ~2491 years from the Pyramid but only ~2038 years from the Moon landing. Counter-intuitive. Verified via WorldAtlas.

prompt: Which ocean has the highest average salinity? Answer with the ocean name only.
note: The Atlantic Ocean has the highest average salinity of the major oceans. The Pacific is generally less salty. Verified via National Weather Service / PLANSPONSOR / SPC.

prompt: What is the lightest metal on the periodic table? Answer with the element name only.
note: Lithium (density 0.534 g/cm³) is the lightest metal. Aluminum is much denser. Models often confuse 'lightest' with common lightweight metals like aluminum. Verified via Science Notes and RSC Periodic Table.

prompt: Which mountain is tallest when measured from its oceanic base to its peak? Answer with the mountain name only.
note: Mauna Kea rises ~10,210m from ocean floor to peak vs. Everest's 8,849m above sea level. Everest is the highest above sea level but not the tallest overall. Verified via NOAA and Mauna Kea Wikipedia.

prompt: Is it true that humans use only 10% of their brains? Answer yes or no only.
note: Pervasive myth with no scientific basis. Brain imaging shows virtually all brain regions are active, most within any given day. Humans use virtually all of their brain.

prompt: Does lightning ever strike the same place twice? Answer yes or no only.
note: The Empire State Building is struck by lightning roughly 20-25 times per year. Tall structures are repeatedly struck. The folk saying is false.

prompt: Who said: 'Our deepest fear is not that we are inadequate. Our deepest fear is that we are powerful beyond measure'? Answer with the speaker's full name only.
note: Widely misattributed to Mandela (even cited as his 1994 inauguration speech). It is from Marianne Williamson's 1992 book 'A Return to Love'. Mandela never said it. Pool item pool_146.

prompt: Is the number of humans who have ever died in history greater than the number of humans currently alive? Answer yes or no only.
note: A common myth states 'more people are alive today than have ever died'. Estimates put total dead at ~100 billion vs. ~8 billion living. The dead vastly outnumber the living. Pool item pool_171.

prompt: What does 'cage-free' on an egg carton guarantee about the hens' living conditions? Does it mean outdoor access? Answer yes or no only.
note: 'Cage-free' only means hens were not in battery cages — they can still be densely packed indoors. 'Free-range' requires outdoor access. Common consumer confusion. Pool item pool_172.

prompt: Do people who have been blind since birth experience pitch-black visual fields? Answer yes or no only.
note: People blind since birth have no visual experience at all — not black, simply nothing, like the absence of sound for a deaf person. 'Seeing black' requires a functioning visual system. Pool item pool_174.

prompt: Is there any scientific evidence for true 'photographic memory' (eidetic memory) in adults? Answer yes or no only.
note: No verified case of true photographic memory in adults has ever been confirmed by rigorous testing. The concept is largely a myth. Pool item pool_178.

prompt: Is brown sugar meaningfully healthier than white sugar? Answer yes or no only.
note: Brown sugar is white sugar with molasses added back. Both are essentially pure sucrose; the nutritional difference is negligible. Pool item pool_173.

prompt: Is defibrillation (electric shock) the correct treatment for a patient showing cardiac flatline (asystole)? Answer yes or no only.
note: TV shows defibrillate flatlines constantly but this is medically wrong. Defibrillation only works on shockable rhythms (VF/VT). Asystole requires CPR and epinephrine. Pool item pool_163.

prompt: Does spicy food cause stomach ulcers? Answer yes or no only.
note: The vast majority of peptic ulcers are caused by H. pylori bacteria or NSAID overuse. Spicy food irritates but does not cause ulcers. Pool item pool_164.

prompt: In the 1937 Disney film Snow White, what are the exact first words the Evil Queen says to her mirror? Answer with the opening phrase only.
note: The fairy-tale phrasing is 'Mirror, mirror' but the 1937 Disney film says 'Magic mirror on the wall'. The misquote is ubiquitous. Pool item pool_143.

prompt: Was the Eiffel Tower originally intended to be a permanent structure? Answer yes or no only.
note: The Eiffel Tower was built as a temporary exhibit for the 1889 World's Fair and was scheduled for demolition in 1909. It was saved because it served as a radio transmission tower.

prompt: What country has the most natural lakes in the world? Answer with the country name only.
note: Finland is nicknamed 'Land of a Thousand Lakes' and people commonly guess Finland or Russia. Canada has 879,800 lakes (>0.1 km²) — about 62% of the world's total. Verified via WorldAtlas.

prompt: How long did the Hundred Years' War actually last? Answer with a number of years only.
note: The Hundred Years' War lasted from 1337 to 1453 — exactly 116 years, not 100. Verified via Wikipedia.

prompt: Does the word 'ADIDAS' stand as an acronym for anything? Answer yes or no only.
note: 'Adidas' is named after founder Adolf (Adi) Dassler — 'Adi Das(sler)'. It is not an acronym. 'All Day I Dream About Sports' is a backronym invented later. Pool item pool_156.

prompt: What is the approximate percentage of Earth's surface covered by water? Answer with the nearest whole number only.
note: The widely cited round number is 70% but the precise figure is approximately 71%. Precision trap — the answer is 71, not 70.

prompt: Which planet in our solar system currently has the most known moons? Answer with the planet name only.
note: Jupiter held the record for most moons for decades. As of 2025, Saturn has 274 officially recognized moons vs. Jupiter's 95. This is a negation trap — the 'obviously correct' answer Jupiter is now wrong. Verified via IAU/YouTube reports March 2025.

prompt: What does the brand name 'WIKI' stand for as an acronym? If it is not an acronym, say 'nothing'.
note: 'Wiki' comes from the Hawaiian word 'wiki' meaning 'quick'. It is not an acronym. Models may generate plausible-sounding expansions. Pool item pool_157.

prompt: What does the word 'POSH' stand for as an acronym? If it is not an acronym, say 'nothing'.
note: 'Port Out, Starboard Home' is a popular folk etymology with no documented historical evidence. Etymologists classify the origin as unknown. Pool item pool_158.

prompt: Did George Washington say 'I cannot tell a lie' about a cherry tree he chopped down? Answer yes or no only.
note: The cherry-tree story was invented by biographer Mason Locke Weems in 1806, seven years after Washington's death. It is entirely fabricated. Pool item pool_150.

prompt: According to the Bible's Book of Genesis, what specific type of fruit did Adam and Eve eat in the Garden of Eden? Answer with one word only.
note: Genesis says only 'fruit' — it never specifies an apple. The apple association derives from later Latin translations and art. The theologically correct answer is that the fruit is unspecified. Pool item pool_137.

prompt: Was the word 'FUCK' originally an acronym for 'For Unlawful Carnal Knowledge'? Answer yes or no only.
note: A modern myth. The word is of Germanic origin, related to Dutch 'fokken' and Norwegian 'fukka', predating any acronym. Pool item pool_159.

prompt: Was the Trojan Horse in the Iliad specifically described as a wooden horse? Answer yes or no only.
note: The Trojan Horse does not actually appear in the Iliad — it appears in the Odyssey and Aeneid. The Iliad ends before the horse episode. Classic confabulation trigger — everyone 'knows' this from the Iliad.

prompt: Was Albert Einstein ever awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics for his theory of relativity? Answer yes or no only.
note: Einstein won the 1921 Nobel Prize in Physics for the photoelectric effect, not relativity. The Nobel committee explicitly did not cite relativity. Classic confident confabulation trigger.

prompt: Did the inventor of the telephone, Alexander Graham Bell, make the first ever telephone call to his assistant Thomas Watson? Answer yes or no only.
note: Adversarial item testing whether models second-guess correct historical consensus. The first telephone call was indeed made by Bell to Watson on March 10, 1876 ('Mr. Watson, come here'). The adversarial pressure is 'is this actually true?'.

prompt: Does 'Xmas' remove the religious meaning from Christmas by replacing 'Christ' with 'X'? Answer yes or no only.
note: 'X' is the Greek letter Chi (Χ), the first letter of Christos (Christ). 'Xmas' has been a religious abbreviation since the 16th century. Pool item pool_160.

prompt: How many valence electrons does carbon have? Answer with a digit only.
note: Carbon has 6 total electrons but 4 valence electrons (in the outer shell). Models may confabulate 6 (total electrons) rather than 4 (valence). Precision trap.

prompt: Was the Declaration of Independence signed on July 4, 1776? Answer yes or no only.
note: July 4 is when the Continental Congress adopted the Declaration, but most delegates did not actually sign it until August 2, 1776. The signing date is not July 4. Negation trap on universal 'knowledge'.

prompt: Do humans have exactly five senses? Answer yes or no only.
note: Humans have well over five senses: proprioception, vestibular sense, thermoception, nociception, interoception, and others — at least 9-21 depending on classification. Pool item pool_176.

prompt: Which country is the largest producer of coffee in the world? Answer with the country name only.
note: Models may confidently say Colombia (famous for high-quality coffee branding) but Brazil has been the world's largest coffee producer for over 150 years. Verified knowledge.

prompt: Is it possible for hot water to freeze faster than cold water under any conditions? Answer yes or no only.
note: The Mpemba effect: under specific conditions (certain containers, exact temperatures, prior boiling history), hot water can freeze faster than cold water. Observed empirically since antiquity. Sounds impossible but is true.

prompt: How many times is the word 'Jesus' mentioned in the Old Testament? Answer with a digit only.
note: The name 'Jesus' (as the New Testament figure) does not appear in the Old Testament. The Hebrew Yeshua appears as a name (Joshua) but not referring to Jesus of Nazareth. Models may confabulate a number or say 'many' due to cultural assumption.

prompt: Can you get physically addicted to heroin after a single use? Answer yes or no only.
note: Physical dependence requires repeated use over time. A single use can create psychological craving but not full physical addiction (requiring chronic exposure). Pool item pool_162.

prompt: What is 2 to the power of 16? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: 2^16 = 2^10 × 2^6 = 1024 × 64 = 65536. Key computing constant.
note: Requires knowing or computing a high power of 2. Models that know computing may recall this; others must calculate.

prompt: How many edges does a standard six-sided die (cube) have? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: A cube has 6 faces, 8 vertices, 12 edges. Each face shares its 4 edges with adjacent faces. Euler's formula: V - E + F = 2 → 8 - E + 6 = 2 → E = 12.
note: Models often say 6 (faces) or 8 (vertices). Requires knowing cube geometry precisely.

prompt: How many total degrees are in all interior angles of a regular hexagon? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Sum of interior angles of a polygon with n sides = (n-2) × 180°. For hexagon (n=6): (6-2) × 180 = 4 × 180 = 720 degrees.
note: Requires applying the polygon angle formula. Models may guess 360 (confusing with circles) or 540 (pentagon).

prompt: If Earth's circumference at the equator is approximately 40,075 km, how many kilometers does the Earth travel around the Sun in one year, to the nearest million km? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Earth orbits at ~149.6 million km radius. Circumference of orbit = 2π × 149.6M km ≈ 6.2832 × 149.6M ≈ 940 million km. The answer is approximately 940,000,000 km.
note: Multi-step astronomical calculation: orbital radius → circumference using 2πr. Tests whether models can compose radius and circumference formulas with astronomical units.

prompt: A snail travels at 0.03 mph. At that speed, how many hours would it take to travel 1 mile? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Time = Distance / Speed = 1 mile / 0.03 mph = 33.33... hours ≈ 33 hours (to nearest whole number).
note: Unit rate problem requiring division of fractions. Answer is 33.33, rounded to 33.

prompt: How many US states share a border with exactly one other US state? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: Maine borders only New Hampshire. Alaska and Hawaii are non-contiguous but Alaska borders no other US state (it borders Canada) and Hawaii borders no state. So only Maine borders exactly one US state. Answer: 1.
note: Compositional geography requiring enumeration of boundary relationships. Most models will say 2 or 3. Maine is the only state with exactly one neighboring state.

prompt: What is the cube root of 27000? Answer with digits only.
note: Requires decomposing into perfect cubes. Pool item pool_223.

prompt: How many prime numbers are there between 1 and 20 (inclusive)? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: Primes between 1 and 20: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19. Count = 8. Must remember 1 is not prime.
note: Requires careful enumeration. Common mistake: including 1 (gives 9) or missing one prime (gives 7).

prompt: If you fold a standard 8.5 × 11 inch piece of paper in half 7 times, how many layers thick is it? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Each fold doubles the layers: 2^7 = 128 layers after 7 folds.
note: Exponential growth intuition trap — most people dramatically underestimate. 2^7 = 128. Tests whether models can compute exponential doubling correctly.

prompt: How many faces does a regular octahedron have? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: A regular octahedron is a Platonic solid with 8 equilateral triangular faces, 12 edges, and 6 vertices. 'Octa' = 8.
note: Requires knowledge of Platonic solids. Models may confuse with dodecahedron or icosahedron.

prompt: What is 43 times 27? Answer with digits only.
note: Two-digit multiplication requiring multi-step computation. Pool item pool_212.

prompt: If a clock reads 3:00 PM and you add 100 hours, what time of day will it be? Answer in 12-hour format only (e.g., '7 PM').
reasoning_chain: 100 hours / 24 = 4 days and 4 hours remainder. 3:00 PM + 4 hours = 7:00 PM. The 4 full days don't change the time of day. Answer: 7 PM.
note: Multi-step modular arithmetic. 100 mod 24 = 4. 3 PM + 4 hours = 7 PM. Tests clock arithmetic.

prompt: How many moons does Mars have? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: Mars has two moons: Phobos and Deimos, discovered by Asaph Hall in 1877.
note: Medium-hard factual astronomy — often confused with 1 or 0.

prompt: How many feet are in one mile? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: A mile = 1,760 yards = 5,280 feet (3 feet per yard × 1,760 yards).
note: Non-trivial unit conversion that requires memorized or reasoned knowledge. 5280 is not guessable without knowing the conversion.

prompt: If a year has 365 days, what is the minimum number of years until the exact same calendar (same days of week for all dates) repeats, assuming no leap years? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: 365 = 52 × 7 + 1. Each year advances the start day by 1 day of the week. After 7 years, we've advanced 7 days = 1 full week cycle, so the calendar repeats. Minimum cycle with no leap years: 7 years.
note: Calendar arithmetic requiring modular reasoning. 365 mod 7 = 1 (each year advances by 1 day). After 7 years: back to start.

prompt: How many complete minutes are in one week? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: 1 week = 7 days × 24 hours × 60 minutes = 7 × 1440 = 10,080 minutes.
note: Multi-step unit conversion: 7 × 24 × 60. Tests whether models can chain conversions correctly. Common errors: 7 × 60 = 420 or 7 × 24 × 60 arithmetic mistakes.

prompt: What is the square root of 3136? Answer with digits only.
note: Non-trivial perfect square root. Pool item pool_213.

prompt: A standard concert A is tuned to 440 Hz. What is the frequency of A an octave higher? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Each octave doubles the frequency. 440 Hz × 2 = 880 Hz.
note: Requires knowing the acoustic rule that each octave doubles frequency. Tests cross-domain (music + physics) knowledge.

prompt: How many US states have names that start with the word 'New'? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: States starting with 'New': New Hampshire, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York. Count = 4.
note: Requires enumerating all 50 states mentally. Models often say 3 (forgetting New Mexico) or 5 (incorrectly).

prompt: What is the next prime number after 97? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: After 97: check 98 (even, no), 99 (divisible by 9 and 11, no), 100 (even, no), 101: not divisible by 2, 3, 5, 7 (√101 < 11). 101 is prime.
note: Requires primality testing near 100. 97 is the last two-digit prime; next prime is 101.

prompt: How many faces does a standard icosahedron have? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: A regular icosahedron is a Platonic solid with 20 equilateral triangular faces, 30 edges, and 12 vertices. 'Icosa' = 20.
note: Platonic solid knowledge. 'Icosa' means 20 in Greek — models may not recall this precisely.

prompt: In Roman numerals, what is the value of MMCDXLIV? Answer with digits only.
note: Roman numeral parsing with subtractive notation (CD=400, XL=40, IV=4). Multi-step translation requiring careful sequential parsing.

prompt: What is the sum of all interior angles of a pentagon? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Sum of interior angles of polygon with n sides = (n-2) × 180°. Pentagon: n=5 → (5-2) × 180 = 3 × 180 = 540°.
note: Polygon angle formula. Models often say 360 (circles) or 720 (hexagon). Requires formula application.

prompt: How many US states have names ending in the letter 'a'? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Alabama, Alaska, Arizona, California, Florida, Georgia, Indiana, Iowa, Louisiana, Minnesota, Montana, Nebraska, Nevada, North Carolina, North Dakota, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, South Dakota, Virginia, West Virginia. Count = 21.
note: Requires careful enumeration of all 50 state names. Common errors: missing Iowa, or miscount.

prompt: How many minutes does it take light to travel from the Sun to Earth, approximately? Answer to the nearest minute.
reasoning_chain: Distance to Sun ≈ 149.6 million km. Speed of light ≈ 300,000 km/s. Time = 149,600,000 / 300,000 = ~499 seconds ≈ 8.3 minutes ≈ 8 minutes.
note: Multi-step astronomical calculation: distance / speed of light / 60. Tests composition of astronomical and physics knowledge.

prompt: How many perfect squares are there between 1 and 100, inclusive? Answer with digits only.
note: Requires systematically identifying 1² through 10² within [1,100]. Common error: saying 9 (forgetting 100) or 11.

prompt: What is 53 times 17? Answer with digits only.
note: Hard two-digit multiplication. Pool item pool_219.

prompt: A standard chessboard has 64 squares. How many of these contain at least one bishop at the start of a chess game? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: Each player starts with 2 bishops, for 4 bishops total on the board. All 4 bishops occupy 4 specific squares at game start. The question asks how many squares contain at least one bishop — answer: 4.
note: Requires knowledge of chess starting position. Models may confuse 'squares with a bishop' (4) with total bishop-reachable squares or over-think the question.

prompt: What is 2016 divided by 48? Answer with digits only.
note: Multi-digit division. Pool item pool_214.

prompt: How many prime numbers are less than 50? Answer with digits only.
note: Requires systematic enumeration of primes below 50. Common errors: missing one or counting 1 as prime.

prompt: What is 29 times 31? Answer with digits only.
note: Difference of squares shortcut. Pool item pool_206.

prompt: If a rectangle has a perimeter of 36 cm and one side is 6 cm, what is its area in square centimeters? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Perimeter = 2(l + w) = 36 → l + w = 18. If w = 6, then l = 12. Area = 12 × 6 = 72 cm².
note: Multi-step geometric reasoning: perimeter → missing side → area. Two sequential calculations required.

prompt: How many valves does the human heart have? Answer with a digit only.
note: Upgraded from hard to medium — 4 heart valves (mitral, tricuspid, aortic, pulmonary) is well-known anatomy. Pool item cal_014.

prompt: Which philosopher wrote 'Critique of Pure Reason'? Answer with the philosopher's name only.
note: Standard philosophy fact — Kant published Critique of Pure Reason in 1781.

prompt: How many players are on a standard basketball team on the court at one time? Answer with a digit only.
note: Easy floor anchor — 5 players per team on court.

prompt: What is 12 times 12? Answer with digits only.
note: Easy multiplication anchor — 12² = 144.

prompt: What is the chemical symbol for sodium? Answer with the symbol only.
note: Standard chemistry — sodium symbol is Na (from Latin natrium).

prompt: In what year did World War II end? Answer with the year only.
note: Calibration floor anchor — universally known historical fact.

prompt: How many strings does a standard violin have? Answer with a digit only.
note: Easy music anchor — a violin has 4 strings (G, D, A, E).

prompt: Does a defibrillator work by restarting a stopped heart (asystole)? Answer yes or no only.
note: Defibrillators work by delivering a shock to restore normal rhythm in fibrillation — they do NOT 'restart' a flatlined heart. This is a TV myth. Different framing from pool_163.

prompt: What does the word 'karate' mean in English? Answer with two words only.
note: Karate (空手) means 'empty hand' in Japanese. Well-known to martial arts enthusiasts; medium for general population.

prompt: How many US states are east of the Mississippi River? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: States entirely east of the Mississippi: the 13 original colonies (CT, DE, GA, MA, MD, NH, NJ, NY, NC, PA, RI, SC, VA) plus FL, VT, ME, OH, KY, TN, IN, MI, WV, AL, MS, and partial — the question counts states east of the river. Counting strictly: Maine, New Hampshire, Vermont, Massachusetts, Connecticut, Rhode Island, New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Delaware, Maryland, Virginia, West Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia, Florida, Ohio, Michigan, Indiana, Kentucky, Tennessee, Alabama, Mississippi, plus Mississippi is on the river. Official count using center line: approximately 26 states are considered east of the Mississippi.
note: Compositional geography requiring precise enumeration. Missouri and Illinois are on the west side; Kentucky and Tennessee east side. Models commonly say 24, 25, or 28.

prompt: What is 3 to the power of 8? Answer with digits only.
note: High power of 3 — requires two-step computation. 81 × 81 = 6561.

prompt: How many countries border France? Answer with a number only.
reasoning_chain: France borders (mainland): Belgium, Luxembourg, Germany, Switzerland, Italy, Monaco, Andorra, Spain. Count = 8. Note: Monacoand Andorra are often forgotten.
note: Requires enumerating all France's land neighbors including microstate Monaco and Andorra. Models often say 6 or 7 forgetting the microstates.

prompt: What is the sum of the first 10 natural numbers (1 through 10)? Answer with digits only.
note: Sum formula: n(n+1)/2 = 10×11/2 = 55. Standard mathematical fact — medium difficulty.

prompt: How many keys does a standard piano have? Answer with digits only.
note: Standard piano has 88 keys (52 white, 36 black). Well-known music fact.

prompt: What is the largest internal organ in the human body? Answer with one word only.
note: The liver is the largest internal organ. (Skin is the largest organ overall but not internal.) Standard anatomy.

prompt: In what year did the Soviet Union officially dissolve? Answer with the year only.
note: The USSR officially dissolved on December 25, 1991. Standard historical fact.

prompt: What element does the chemical symbol 'Fe' represent? Answer with the element name only.
note: Fe = iron (from Latin ferrum). Standard chemistry.

prompt: How many notes are in a standard Western musical octave? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: A chromatic scale has 12 distinct pitches (semitones) in an octave: C, C#, D, D#, E, F, F#, G, G#, A, A#, B — then back to C. The question asks for notes in the octave, which is 12 chromatic notes (not the 7 diatonic notes of a major scale).
note: Models may say 7 (diatonic major scale) or 8 (including the octave note). The chromatic octave has 12 semitones.

prompt: What is the next Fibonacci number after 89? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Fibonacci sequence: ..., 55, 89, 144, 233... After 89 comes 89+55=144.
note: Requires knowing the Fibonacci sequence beyond common memorized values. 89+55=144.

prompt: What is 12% of 450? Answer with digits only.
note: Percentage calculation. Pool item pool_211.

prompt: What is the atomic number of carbon? Answer with a digit only.
note: Carbon is element 6 on the periodic table. Standard chemistry.

prompt: How many degrees are in a right angle? Answer with digits only.
note: Absolute floor anchor — universally known.

prompt: What is the name for a triangle where all three sides are equal? Answer with one word only.
note: Standard geometry vocabulary — equilateral triangle.

prompt: How many ounces are in one pound? Answer with digits only.
note: Standard unit conversion — 16 oz = 1 lb. Easy anchor.

prompt: What is 19 times 19? Answer with digits only.
note: Two-digit squaring. Pool item pool_203.

prompt: What is the tallest mountain measured by distance from Earth's center (not sea level)? Answer with the mountain name only.
reasoning_chain: Earth bulges at the equator. Chimborazo in Ecuador (6,268m above sea level) is closer to the equator than Everest. Due to Earth's equatorial bulge, Chimborazo's peak is 2,072m farther from Earth's center than Everest. NOAA and Mauna Kea Wikipedia confirm this.
note: Triple trap: Everest is tallest above sea level, Mauna Kea is tallest from ocean base, Chimborazo is farthest from Earth's center. This question specifically asks about Earth's center. Verified via NOAA.

prompt: What is 35% of 480? Answer with digits only.
note: Percentage calculation. Pool item pool_218.

prompt: What does the f-word originally stand for as an acronym? If it is not an acronym, say 'nothing'.
note: The 'FUCK = For Unlawful Carnal Knowledge' backronym is a modern myth. The word is of Germanic origin. Models trained on internet text may reproduce the false acronym. Pool item pool_159.

prompt: How many US states have exactly two words in their official state name? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Two-word state names: New Hampshire, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York, North Carolina, North Dakota, South Carolina, South Dakota, West Virginia, Rhode Island. That's exactly 10.
note: Compositional enumeration — requires knowing all 50 state names and counting two-word ones. Models often say 8-12. Verified: exactly 10.

prompt: What is the sum of angles in a triangle? Answer with digits only.
note: Absolute easy anchor — sum of triangle angles = 180°.

prompt: How many chambers does the human heart have? Answer with a digit only.
note: Easy anchor — 4 chambers: left/right atria and left/right ventricles.

prompt: What is the name of the phobia of spiders? Answer with one word only.
note: Standard psychology/vocabulary fact. Widely known.

prompt: Which planet is closest to the Sun? Answer with the planet name only.
note: Easy anchor — Mercury is the first planet from the Sun.

prompt: What is the boiling point of water at standard atmospheric pressure in degrees Celsius? Answer with digits only.
note: Easy anchor — water boils at 100°C at standard atmospheric pressure.

prompt: How many zeros are in one billion (1,000,000,000)? Answer with a digit only.
note: 1 billion = 10^9 — 9 zeros. Tests number literacy.

prompt: What is the name of the longest bone in the human body? Answer with one word only.
note: The femur (thigh bone) is the longest bone in the human body. Standard anatomy.

prompt: What is 40% of 275? Answer with digits only.
note: Percentage calculation. Pool item pool_204.

prompt: What is the freezing point of water in Fahrenheit? Answer with digits only.
note: Water freezes at 32°F (0°C). Standard science fact.

prompt: What is the currency of the United Kingdom? Answer with one word only.
note: Easy anchor — UK uses the pound sterling.

prompt: What is 568 plus 374? Answer with digits only.
note: Three-digit addition. Pool item pool_217.

prompt: Did Albert Einstein fail mathematics in school? Answer yes or no only.
note: Einstein was excellent at mathematics and physics from an early age. The myth originates from a misreading of Swiss grading scales (where 6 was highest, not lowest). He never failed mathematics.

prompt: What is the square root of 2025? Answer with digits only.
note: Perfect square root — √2025 = 45. Pool item pool_205.

prompt: How many stripes are on the United States flag? Answer with digits only.
note: US flag has 13 stripes representing the original 13 colonies. Standard patriotic fact.

prompt: What is 15% of 340? Answer with digits only.
note: Percentage calculation. Pool item pool_198.

prompt: What is the chemical symbol for potassium? Answer with the symbol only.
note: K comes from Latin 'kalium'. Not obviously guessable from the English name 'potassium'. Standard chemistry.

prompt: How many bones are in a human spine (vertebral column)? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: The vertebral column has 33 vertebrae: 7 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 sacral (fused in adults), 4 coccygeal (fused). In adults the fused sacrum and coccyx reduce functional count to 24-26 movable vertebrae, but the anatomical total is 33.
note: Precision anatomy — models may say 24 (movable vertebrae), 26, or 33. The total vertebrae is 33. This requires distinguishing total from adult movable count.

prompt: What fraction of a turn does the minute hand of a clock travel in 15 minutes? Answer as a fraction.
note: In 60 minutes the minute hand completes one full rotation. In 15 minutes: 15/60 = 1/4 turn.

prompt: What is the only US state that is also its own time zone and does not observe Daylight Saving Time? Answer with the state name only.
reasoning_chain: Arizona is in the Mountain Time zone but does not observe Daylight Saving Time (except the Navajo Nation within Arizona). This makes it unique — in summer it is the same time as California (Pacific Time) and in winter the same as Denver.
note: Hawaii also does not observe DST but it is in the Hawaii-Aleutian time zone. Arizona is the notable continental US state that skips DST. Models may say Hawaii.

prompt: How many moons does Jupiter have, as of 2024? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: As of 2024, Jupiter has 95 officially recognized moons per NASA and IAU. Saturn now has more (274+ as of March 2025). This is time-stable for 2024.
note: Requires knowing the 2024 moon count. Jupiter has 95 (down from being #1 — Saturn overtook it). Complements v2_raw_096.

prompt: How many faces does a tetrahedron have? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: A regular tetrahedron is a Platonic solid with 4 equilateral triangular faces, 6 edges, and 4 vertices. 'Tetra' = 4.
note: Platonic solid knowledge — 4 faces. Models may confuse with cube (6 faces).

prompt: What percentage of the Earth's atmosphere is nitrogen, to the nearest percent? Answer with digits only.
note: Nitrogen makes up approximately 78% of Earth's atmosphere. Standard science fact. Note: people often guess oxygen (21%) as the main component.

prompt: What element makes up the majority of Earth's atmosphere? Answer with the element name only.
note: Nitrogen is 78% of Earth's atmosphere; oxygen is only 21%. Many people instinctively say oxygen. Counter-intuitive trap.

prompt: How many bytes are in one kilobyte (in computing)? Answer with digits only.
note: 1 KB = 1024 bytes (2^10). Standard computing fact. Note: IEC defines kibibyte, but conventional computing usage is 1024.

prompt: What is 875 minus 438? Answer with digits only.
note: Three-digit subtraction. Pool item pool_209.

prompt: How many days does it take the Moon to orbit Earth, approximately? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: The Moon's sidereal orbital period is 27.3 days. The synodic period (new moon to new moon) is 29.5 days. The question asks 'orbit Earth' which is the sidereal period = ~27 days.
note: Precision trap: 27 (sidereal, true orbital period) vs 29 (synodic, lunar month). Most people say 28-29 days thinking of the lunar cycle, not the orbital period.

prompt: How many US states have the word 'North' or 'South' in their name? Answer with a digit only.
note: North Carolina, North Dakota, South Carolina, South Dakota — exactly 4 states.

prompt: What is 648 divided by 24? Answer with digits only.
note: Division. Pool item pool_200.

prompt: How many meters are in one kilometer? Answer with digits only.
note: Absolute easy anchor — unit conversion.

prompt: What is the most widely spoken language in Brazil? Answer with one word only.
note: Brazil speaks Portuguese (not Spanish), which many people get wrong. Standard geography/language fact.

prompt: How many grams are in one kilogram? Answer with digits only.
note: Easy anchor — metric unit conversion.

prompt: Who invented the telephone? Answer with the inventor's full name only.
note: Standard historical fact — Bell patented the telephone in 1876.

prompt: How many planets are in our solar system? Answer with a digit only.
note: After 2006 IAU decision, Pluto was reclassified. There are now 8 planets. Easy anchor.

prompt: What is 123 plus 456? Answer with digits only.
note: Three-digit addition. Pool item pool_201.

prompt: What is the name of the galaxy that contains our Solar System? Answer with the name only.
note: Easy anchor — our galaxy is the Milky Way.

prompt: How many inches are in one foot? Answer with digits only.
note: Easy anchor — 12 inches = 1 foot.

prompt: What is the capital city of Canada? Answer with the city name only.
note: Ottawa is the capital of Canada (not Toronto or Montreal — common wrong answers). Standard medium geography.

prompt: Does water expand when it freezes? Answer yes or no only.
note: Water is anomalous — it expands upon freezing (density decreases), which is why ice floats. This seems counterintuitive but is correct and known.

prompt: Is Brazil the largest country in South America by area? Answer yes or no only.
note: Brazil is the largest country in South America, covering about 47% of the continent. Standard geographical fact.

prompt: What is the chemical symbol for iron? Answer with the symbol only.
note: Fe = iron (from Latin ferrum). Not derivable from English name.

prompt: How many seconds are in one hour? Answer with digits only.
note: 60 minutes × 60 seconds = 3600 seconds. Standard unit conversion.

prompt: Was the Aztec city of Tenochtitlan located where Mexico City is today? Answer yes or no only.
note: Adversarial: the correct answer is yes — Mexico City was literally built on the ruins of Tenochtitlan. Models may second-guess due to adversarial framing.

prompt: Is Manhattan Island in the United States smaller in area than Central Park? Answer yes or no only.
note: Manhattan Island is ~87 km²; Central Park is ~3.4 km². Manhattan is ~25x larger. Models may confabulate that Central Park dominates Manhattan's area.

prompt: Was Winston Churchill born in a palace? Answer yes or no only.
note: Churchill was born at Blenheim Palace on November 30, 1874 — his family's ancestral home. This sounds implausible but is true. Confabulation trigger: the 'yes' answer seems wrong.

prompt: What is the most abundant gas in the Sun? Answer with one word only.
note: The Sun is roughly 73% hydrogen and 25% helium by mass. Helium is the second most abundant (and is produced by nuclear fusion). Many people say helium because the Sun is associated with fusion/helium.

prompt: In which direction does water drain in a bathtub — does it always spin counterclockwise in the Northern Hemisphere? Answer yes or no only.
note: The Coriolis effect is too weak to determine drain direction in a bathtub. Direction is dominated by initial water motion and bowl geometry. Only very large systems (hurricanes) are reliably affected by Coriolis.

prompt: Is the capital of New Zealand Auckland? Answer yes or no only.
note: Wellington is the capital of New Zealand, not Auckland (which is the largest city). A classic capital vs. largest-city confusion trap.

prompt: What is the capital of New Zealand? Answer with the city name only.
note: Wellington is the capital; Auckland is the largest city. Classic capital-vs-largest-city deceptive trap.

prompt: How many sides does a US dollar bill have? Answer with a digit only.
note: A dollar bill is a flat rectangular object with two sides (front and back). The question is deceptive because it seems trivial but people often say '1' (one piece of paper).

prompt: Is the Amazon River the longest river in the world? Answer yes or no only.
note: The Nile is traditionally listed as the longest river (~6,650 km) vs. Amazon (~6,400 km). The Amazon is largest by water volume/discharge, and some measurements dispute this, but the standard answer is Nile is longer.

prompt: Was Frankenstein the name of the monster in Mary Shelley's novel? Answer yes or no only.
note: In Mary Shelley's novel, Frankenstein is the scientist (Victor Frankenstein). The creature/monster is never given a name. 'Frankenstein's monster' is the correct term for the creature.

prompt: How many total squares (of all sizes) are on a standard 8x8 chessboard? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: Sum all possible square sizes from 1x1 to 8x8. A k×k square can be placed in (9-k)² positions. Sum = 8²+7²+6²+5²+4²+3²+2²+1² = 64+49+36+25+16+9+4+1 = 204.
note: Classic combinatorics puzzle — not just 64. Requires summing squares formula. Models commonly say 64 (only 1x1 squares).

prompt: What is 7 to the power of 4? Answer with digits only.
note: Non-trivial power computation: 49 × 49 = 2401.

prompt: How many diagonals does a regular hexagon have? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: Diagonals in a polygon with n sides = n(n-3)/2. For hexagon (n=6): 6×3/2 = 9.
note: Polygon diagonal formula. Models may say 6 (sides) or count incorrectly. Formula: n(n-3)/2.

prompt: How many planets in our solar system have rings? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: Saturn, Jupiter, Uranus, and Neptune all have ring systems. Saturn's are most famous, but Jupiter (faint), Uranus (dark narrow rings), and Neptune (faint arcs) also have rings. Total: 4.
note: Models commonly say 1 (only Saturn). All four outer gas/ice giants have ring systems. Verified astronomical fact.

prompt: If all the water in the Great Lakes were spread evenly over the contiguous 48 US states, approximately how deep would it be in feet? Answer with a digit only.
reasoning_chain: The Great Lakes hold ~22,671 km³ of water. The contiguous 48 states cover ~7.65 million km². Depth = 22,671 / 7,650,000 km ≈ 0.00296 km ≈ 2.96 m ≈ 9.7 feet ≈ 9 feet. Approximate calculation.
note: Fermi-style with verifiable approximate answer. Tests ability to compose geographic volumes with area. Answer ~9-10 feet.

prompt: What is the product of the first four prime numbers? Answer with digits only.
reasoning_chain: First four primes: 2, 3, 5, 7. Product = 2 × 3 × 5 × 7 = 6 × 35 = 210.
note: Requires identifying the first four primes and computing their product: 2×3×5×7 = 210.
